comparisons.left: A stepwise evaluation and treatment approach is recommended for acute or subacute uncomplicated TBAD, including confirmation that malperfusion and other indications of complicated disease are absent.
comparisons.right: For acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD, STS/AATS recommends a stepwise evaluation that identifies the primary entry tear, defines its relationship to the left subclavian artery, measures maximum orthogonal aortic diameter, and confirms absence of malperfusion or other complicated-disease indications.

comparisons.left: A stepwise approach to the evaluation and treatment of acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD should be applied that includes identification of the primary entry tear site location, defining the proximity and distance of the dissection to the LSA, calibration of the maximum orthogonal aortic diameter, and confirmation of the lack of any organ malperfusion or other indications of complicated disease.
comparisons.right: A stepwise approach to the evaluation and treatment of acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD should be applied that includes identification of the primary entry tear site location, defining the proximity and distance of the dissection to the LSA, calibration of the maximum orthogonal aortic diameter, and confirmation of the lack of any organ malperfusion or other indications of complicated disease.

comparisons.right: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7 | Uncomplicated TBAD | Formal recommendation bullet 1 | COR I; LOE B-NR. Exact recommendation wording preserved.

left: The Class IIB prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation states that TEVAR may be considered; it does not recommend routine TEVAR for every patient with uncomplicated TBAD.

The prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation does not print a separate acuity qualifier, while the stepwise recommendation explicitly specifies acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD and the supporting INSTEAD population spans subacute and chronic phases.

The guideline defines TBAD with listed high-risk features as a category distinct from uncomplicated TBAD, while its treatment-paradigm text discusses stable patients with high-risk features; specialist interpretation is required to reconcile terminology when applying the recommendation.
The guideline summarizes INSTEAD, INSTEAD-XL, and ADSORB, but those publications remain the primary sources for numerical outcomes, population definitions, and risk-of-bias assessment.

Favorable remodeling is not proof of mortality benefit. The guideline reports no two-year all-cause or aorta-related mortality difference in INSTEAD and states that longer-term ADSORB outcome comparisons were still needed.

The appended 2023 erratum changes reference number 92 to reference number 140 in a sentence about retrograde type A dissection after TEVAR; it does not change the recommendation text extracted here or the identity of the 2022 guideline.
right: The guideline defines 'uncomplicated' TBAD as lacking high-risk features, while later passages discuss 'acute uncomplicated TBAD with high-risk features.' This terminology tension must remain visible and requires specialist interpretation.

The narrative high-risk list on printed p. 1077 includes an entry tear on the lesser curvature, whereas Table 1 on printed p. 1080 lists a primary entry tear at the greater curve of the distal arch. The source does not reconcile these statements.
Table 1 is titled 'Morphologic Features Posing High Risk of Late Sequelae'; it does not establish that each feature predicts benefit from prophylactic or early TEVAR.
Stent graft-induced new entry is a post-intervention feature and is not a baseline selection criterion for first-time TEVAR.

Several recommendations rely on nonrandomized evidence or limited data. The guideline explicitly identifies lack of high-quality randomized evidence for optimal intervention timing and routine TEVAR in uncomplicated TBAD.
The recommendation for prophylactic TEVAR says 'may be considered' and does not recommend TEVAR for every uncomplicated patient.
No numerical outcome records were created because the diameter and timing values extracted here are selection thresholds or recommendation parameters, not directly reported clinical outcome results.
The appended 2023 erratum concerns a reference number in the connective-tissue-disorders section and does not alter the Q03 content extracted here.

differences.left: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7 | Uncomplicated TBAD | Second recommendation under Uncomplicated TBAD; COR I; LOE B-NR.
differences.right: Printed p. 1079; PDF p. 7 | Uncomplicated TBAD | Formal recommendation bullet 2 | COR I; LOE B-NR. Baseline-management context; this is not a high-risk selection criterion.

differences.left: Direct
differences.right: Context only

left: The Class IIB prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation states that TEVAR may be considered; it does not recommend routine TEVAR for every patient with uncomplicated TBAD.

The prophylactic-TEVAR recommendation does not print a separate acuity qualifier, while the stepwise recommendation explicitly specifies acute/subacute uncomplicated TBAD and the supporting INSTEAD population spans subacute and chronic phases.

The guideline defines TBAD with listed high-risk features as a category distinct from uncomplicated TBAD, while its treatment-paradigm text discusses stable patients with high-risk features; specialist interpretation is required to reconcile terminology when applying the recommendation.
The guideline summarizes INSTEAD, INSTEAD-XL, and ADSORB, but those publications remain the primary sources for numerical outcomes, population definitions, and risk-of-bias assessment.

Favorable remodeling is not proof of mortality benefit. The guideline reports no two-year all-cause or aorta-related mortality difference in INSTEAD and states that longer-term ADSORB outcome comparisons were still needed.

The appended 2023 erratum changes reference number 92 to reference number 140 in a sentence about retrograde type A dissection after TEVAR; it does not change the recommendation text extracted here or the identity of the 2022 guideline.
right: The guideline defines 'uncomplicated' TBAD as lacking high-risk features, while later passages discuss 'acute uncomplicated TBAD with high-risk features.' This terminology tension must remain visible and requires specialist interpretation.

The narrative high-risk list on printed p. 1077 includes an entry tear on the lesser curvature, whereas Table 1 on printed p. 1080 lists a primary entry tear at the greater curve of the distal arch. The source does not reconcile these statements.
Table 1 is titled 'Morphologic Features Posing High Risk of Late Sequelae'; it does not establish that each feature predicts benefit from prophylactic or early TEVAR.
Stent graft-induced new entry is a post-intervention feature and is not a baseline selection criterion for first-time TEVAR.

Several recommendations rely on nonrandomized evidence or limited data. The guideline explicitly identifies lack of high-quality randomized evidence for optimal intervention timing and routine TEVAR in uncomplicated TBAD.
The recommendation for prophylactic TEVAR says 'may be considered' and does not recommend TEVAR for every uncomplicated patient.
No numerical outcome records were created because the diameter and timing values extracted here are selection thresholds or recommendation parameters, not directly reported clinical outcome results.
The appended 2023 erratum concerns a reference number in the connective-tissue-disorders section and does not alter the Q03 content extracted here.

comparisons.left: Prophylactic TEVAR may be considered in uncomplicated TBAD to reduce late aortic-related adverse events and aortic-related death.
comparisons.right: For acute uncomplicated TBAD with high-risk features, STS/AATS states that delaying TEVAR beyond 24 hours and up to 90 days may be reasonable to reduce early adverse events and improve late outcomes.

comparisons.left: Prophylactic TEVAR may be considered in patients with uncomplicated TBAD to reduce late aortic-related adverse events and aortic-related death.
comparisons.right: In patients with acute uncomplicated TBAD with high-risk features, it may be reasonable to consider delaying treatment (beyond 24 hours up to 90 days) with TEVAR to reduce early adverse events and to improve late outcomes.

The guideline defines acute as within 2 weeks, subacute as through 3 months, and chronic thereafter, while its 'early chronic' preemptive-TEVAR window extends through 1 year after onset. Other societies subdivide or label these intervals differently.

Table 41 recommends medical treatment for uncomplicated acute-phase patients (Class I, Level C), selective preemptive TEVAR in subacute-to-early-chronic high-risk cases (Class IIa, Level B), and says acute-phase preemptive TEVAR may be considered (Class IIb, Level B). These distinct strengths and time periods must not be collapsed.

The guideline uses an IRAD-derived five-item definition of complicated dissection that includes persistent/recurrent pain, uncontrollable hypertension, and large or rapidly enlarging aortic diameter, while noting that SVS/STS reporting standards classify the latter features as high-risk rather than complicated.
